description: The currently displayed image is an irrelevant non-scientific icon, which contains a two-color (cyan and yellow) circular ring, a red bookmark shape inside the ring, and the text "Check for updates" below the pattern. No valid scientific content related to the research on near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steels, such as microstructure characterization images, mechanical property curves, phase analysis results, or material processing flow diagrams, can be identified from this image. No information supporting the core conclusions about the effects of Cr addition on the microstructure, mechanical properties and deformation behavior of the studied steels can be extracted from the provided invalid image.

This figure consists of two sets of tensile mechanical property curves and three transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs, investigating Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C low-density steels with 0, 3, 6 wt.% Cr (denoted 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr) before and after 20% reduction cold rolling. The upper two plots display true stress (solid lines, left y-axis, unit MPa) and strain hardening rate (dσ/dε, dashed lines, right y-axis, unit MPa) against true strain, with the left plot for pre-cold rolling steels and the right for 20% cold-rolled steels. The lower three TEM bright-field images (scale bar 500 nm) with [110] selected area electron diffraction patterns correspond to cold-rolled 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr steels respectively, showing {111} slip bands whose spacing decreases with increasing Cr content. Key trends: after cold rolling, ultimate tensile strength and strain hardening rate rise with increasing Cr content, while elongation slightly decreases. This figure supports that higher Cr addition promotes formation of finer slip bands during cold rolling, enhancing strength and strain hardening ability via stronger dislocation hindrance effect.

This figure displays X-ray diffraction patterns of low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steels (x=0, 3, 6, denoted as 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr respectively) fabricated via near-rapid solidification. Subplot (a) shows XRD profiles of as-cast steel strips, while subplot (b) shows profiles of strips after 600℃ annealing for 3 hours. The horizontal axis is diffraction angle 2θ ranging from 30° to 100°, and the vertical axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary units. Detected phases include face-centered cubic γ austenite, body-centered cubic δ ferrite, and ordered DO3 phase, with corresponding characteristic peaks labeled. The intensity of δ ferrite/DO3 phase peaks rises with increasing Cr content, indicating higher volume fraction of δ ferrite in higher-Cr alloys. After annealing, more δ ferrite transforms into DO3 ordered phase, especially for 6Cr steel. This figure verifies that Cr addition promotes δ ferrite formation, and annealing facilitates δ ferrite to DO3 phase transition in this steel system.

This set of SEM micrographs shows the microstructures of near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steels, with a uniform 20 μm scale bar for all subgraphs. Subgraphs (a), (b), (c) correspond to as-cast 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr steel strips respectively, while (d), (e), (f) correspond to 600℃ annealed 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr steel strips. The γ austenite matrix and δ ferrite phase are marked in the images. It can be observed that the volume fraction of δ ferrite increases with the rise of Cr content in as-cast samples, and its morphology evolves from short rod-like shape to irregular block-like shape. After annealing, part of δ ferrite transforms into DO3 ordered phase, and samples with higher Cr content retain higher volume fraction of δ/DO3 phase. This figure confirms that Cr addition promotes the formation of δ ferrite, providing microstructural basis for the mechanical property differences of the studied steels.

This figure presents mechanical tensile property curves of near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%, denoted as 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr) steels in two processing states: as-cast and annealed (600℃ for 3 h). Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves of as-cast steels, (b) shows engineering stress-strain curves of annealed steels, (c) presents true stress-strain curves (solid lines) and corresponding strain hardening rate curves (dashed lines) of as-cast steels, and (d) shows true stress-strain and strain hardening rate curves of annealed steels. For as-cast samples, yield strength decreases with increasing Cr content, while 6Cr steel has the highest ultimate tensile strength (~1000 MPa) and comparable elongation to 3Cr steel. After annealing, yield strength of 0Cr steel increases to ~967 MPa (higher than Cr-containing steels), while all samples retain ultimate tensile strength of ~1000-1030 MPa. Strain hardening rate rises with increasing Cr content in both processing states, and annealed 0Cr steel exhibits a distinct double-hump strain hardening rate feature. These results demonstrate that Cr addition reduces κ-carbide precipitation, modifying yield strength and strain hardening behavior of the low-density steels.

This set of TEM characterizations targets the microstructures of as-cast Cr-free (0Cr) Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C low-density steel. Subfigure (a) is the bright-field (BF) image of γ-austenite, showing nanoscale precipitates distributed in the austenite matrix. Subfigure (b) is the dark-field (DF) image captured using the (001) diffraction spot, highlighting the nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates. Subfigure (c) is the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SADP) along the [110] zone axis of γ-austenite, confirming the presence of ordered κ-carbides with orientation relationship [110]γ//[110]κ, with average size 6 nm and volume fraction 3%. Subfigure (d) is the BF image of δ-ferrite, subfigure (e) is the DF image using the (002̅) superlattice reflection, showing ordered phases in δ-ferrite, and subfigure (f) is the SADP of δ-ferrite along [110] zone axis, verifying the ordered phase is DO3 phase with average size 26±3 nm. This figure confirms that nanoscale κ-carbides precipitate in γ-austenite and DO3 ordered phases form in δ-ferrite in as-cast 0Cr low-density steel.

This set of TEM characterization targets as-cast Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-3Cr low-density steel strip. Subfigure (a) is the bright-field (BF) image of γ-austenite, marked as γ+κ phase region, with a scale bar of 500 nm. Subfigure (b) is the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SADP) of γ+κ region along [110] zone axis, with diffraction spots including 1̄10, 1̄11, 001, 002 indexed. Subfigure (c) is the BF image of δ-ferrite, marked as δ+DO3 ordered phase region, with a scale bar of 500 nm. Subfigure (d) is the dark-field (DF) image captured using the (002̄) superlattice reflection of DO3 phase, showing nano-sized DO3 precipitates, with a scale bar of 500 nm. Subfigure (e) is the SADP of δ+DO3 region along [110] zone axis, containing both matrix spots and superlattice spots of ordered DO3 phase. The figure directly verifies that κ-carbide coexists with austenite, and DO3 ordered phase coexists with ferrite in as-cast 3Cr steel, providing microstructural evidence for phase composition analysis of Cr-containing low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This set of TEM characterizations targets the as-cast Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-6Cr low-density steel fabricated via near-rapid solidification, focusing on the microstructures of γ-austenite and δ-ferrite regions. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM image of γ-austenite with a 100 nm scale bar. Subfigure (b) is the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SADP) of γ-austenite along the [110] zone axis, containing diffraction spots from both austenite and κ-carbide, verifying the coexistence of γ matrix and nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates. Subfigure (c) is the BF TEM image of the δ-ferrite region marked as δ+DO3, with a 500 nm scale bar. Subfigure (d) is the dark-field (DF) TEM image collected using the (002̄) superlattice reflection of the DO3 ordered phase, showing the uniform distribution of nanoscale DO3 precipitates in the δ-ferrite matrix. Subfigure (e) is the SADP of δ-ferrite along the [110] zone axis, containing diffraction spots from both δ-ferrite and DO3 ordered phase, confirming the presence of DO3 ordered precipitates in δ-ferrite. This figure provides direct evidence for the phase constitutions and precipitate characteristics in as-cast 6Cr steel, supporting the conclusion that Cr addition promotes DO3 ordered phase formation in δ-ferrite.

This figure contains two sets of X-ray diffraction patterns for Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6, denoted as 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr respectively) low-density steel strips. Subplot (a) shows patterns for samples subjected to 20% cold rolling (CR20%), while subplot (b) shows patterns for CR20% samples further annealed at 600℃ for 3h. The x-axis is diffraction angle 2θ ranging from 30° to 100°, and the y-axis is diffraction intensity in arbitrary units. Identified phases include γ austenite (with labeled peaks (111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, (311)γ, (222)γ), δ ferrite (with labeled peaks (110)δ, (200)δ, (211)δ), and ordered DO3 phase (with labeled peak (220)DO3). As Cr content increases from 0 to 6 wt.%, the intensity of δ ferrite/DO3 phase peaks increases, demonstrating that Cr addition promotes δ ferrite formation and its transformation to DO3 ordered phase. After post-cold rolling annealing, δ ferrite is nearly fully converted to DO3 phase. This figure verifies the regulatory effect of Cr addition and annealing treatment on the phase composition of the studied low-density steels.

This set of 6 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs displays the microstructures of 20% cold-rolled (CR20%) and CR20% followed by 600 °C annealing for 3 h Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steels, with a uniform scale bar of 20 μm. Subfigures (a), (b), (c) correspond to CR20% samples with 0 wt.% Cr, 3 wt.% Cr, 6 wt.% Cr respectively; (d), (e), (f) correspond to CR20%+annealed samples with 0 wt.% Cr, 3 wt.% Cr, 6 wt.% Cr respectively. RD (rolling direction) and TD (transverse direction) are marked for orientation reference. The microstructures consist of gray γ austenite matrix and bright irregular δ ferrite phases distributed in the matrix. As Cr content increases, the volume fraction of δ ferrite rises, and its morphology evolves from short rod-like to more irregular block-like. The figure illustrates the effects of Cr addition and post-cold rolling annealing on phase morphology and distribution of the duplex low-density steel, supporting the conclusion that Cr addition promotes δ ferrite formation.

This figure presents mechanical tensile properties of Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%, denoted as 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr) low-density steels under two processing conditions: 20% cold rolled (CR20%) and CR20% followed by 600 °C annealing for 3 h. Subplots (a) and (b) show engineering stress-strain curves for CR20% and CR20%+annealed steels respectively, while (c) and (d) display corresponding true stress-strain curves (solid lines) and strain hardening rate curves (dashed lines) for the two processed states. It is observed that strength and strain hardening rate increase with rising Cr content in CR20% steels: 3Cr and 6Cr CR20% steels exhibit higher ultimate tensile strength (~1270 MPa, ~1264 MPa respectively) than 0Cr CR20% steel (~1154 MPa). Annealing treatment leads to an obvious decrease of early-stage strain hardening rate for 0Cr and 3Cr steels, and does not significantly improve the yield strength of all three steels. These results support that higher density of slip bands in Cr-containing cold-rolled steels blocks dislocation movement, leading to enhanced strength.

This set of TEM bright-field micrographs show the γ-austenite microstructures of 20% cold-rolled Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steels, where subfigure (a) corresponds to 0Cr steel, (b) to 3Cr steel, and (c) to 6Cr steel. Each subfigure includes a selected area electron diffraction pattern taken from the [110] zone axis of γ-austenite at the top right, with a unified scale bar of 500 nm. {111} planar slip bands are observed in the austenite of all three cold-rolled samples. The average slip band spacing decreases significantly with increasing Cr content, measured as 371 ± 47 nm for 0Cr steel, 100 ± 15 nm for 3Cr steel, and 45 ± 13 nm for 6Cr steel. The figure confirms that higher Cr content refines slip bands after cold rolling, and smaller slip band spacing leads to stronger cold rolling reinforcement effect, supporting the conclusion that Cr addition improves the mechanical performance of cold-rolled low-density steels.

This set of 6 TEM micrographs characterizes γ-austenite phase in annealed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%) low-density steel strips. (a) is the bright-field (BF) image of γ-austenite in 0Cr steel with 500 nm scale bar, accompanied by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern along [110] zone axis marking the (001) superlattice reflection of κ-carbides. (b) is the dark-field (DF) image from the (001) superlattice reflection of 0Cr steel, showing nano-sized κ-carbides with average size of 8 nm and volume fraction of 7%. (c) is the BF image of γ-austenite in 3Cr steel with 100 nm scale bar, accompanied by the same SAED pattern. (d) is the corresponding DF image of 3Cr steel, showing κ-carbides with average size of 4 nm and volume fraction of 4%. (e) is the BF image of γ-austenite in 6Cr steel with 100 nm scale bar, accompanied by SAED pattern. (f) is the corresponding DF image of 6Cr steel with nearly no κ-carbide precipitation. The results demonstrate that increasing Cr content significantly reduces the size and amount of κ-carbide precipitates in γ-austenite of annealed steels, as Cr increases the solid solubility of Al and C in the matrix to reduce the driving force for κ-carbide formation, which further decreases the yield strength of annealed Cr-containing steels.

This set of TEM micrographs characterize γ-austenite in 20% cold-rolled (CR20%) and 600℃ annealed low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%) steel strips. Subfigures (a), (c), (e) are bright-field (BF) images of γ-austenite in 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr steel strips respectively, with inset selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns collected along [110] zone axis confirming the crystal orientation. Subfigures (b), (d), (f) are dark-field (DF) images obtained using (001) superlattice reflection to visualize κ-carbide precipitates. κ-carbides are observed to precipitate mainly at dislocation entanglements in 0Cr and 3Cr samples, while κ-carbides in 6Cr sample are too small to be detected in the DF image, indicating Cr addition suppresses the precipitation and growth of κ-carbides. The figure supports that annealing-induced κ-carbides have negligible effect on yield strength improvement, as high-density slip bands introduced by cold rolling act as the dominant strengthening factor for the cold-rolled steel strips.

This set of TEM micrographs presents deformation microstructures of annealed near-rapidly solidified low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steels at a true tensile strain of 0.075, with a 500 nm scale bar for all subgraphs. Subfigure (a) shows slip bands aligned along {111} planes and dislocation arrays in 0Cr steel, accompanied by a [110] selected area electron diffraction pattern; (b) exhibits the dislocation network structure formed in deformed 0Cr steel; (c) displays slip bands along {111} planes in 6Cr steel with significantly smaller spacing than that in 0Cr steel, also attached with a [110] selected area electron diffraction pattern; (d) reveals scattered dislocations with local entanglement and agglomeration in deformed 6Cr steel. The observation confirms that 6 wt.% Cr addition increases slip band density at the same strain, which hinders dislocation glide and improves strain hardening rate, supporting the mechanism of Cr-modulated deformation behavior in the studied low-density steels.

This set of figures presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs and corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of annealed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C low-density steels with 0 wt% Cr (0Cr) and 6 wt% Cr (6Cr) after tensile fracture. Subfigure (a) shows {111} plane slip bands in 0Cr steel, with an inset SAED pattern aligned along the [110] zone axis. Subfigure (b) displays intersections between ferrite and microbands in 0Cr steel. Subfigure (c) presents a shearable grain boundary in 6Cr steel, with inset SAED patterns from both sides of the boundary both indexed to the [110] zone axis, confirming it is a low-angle grain boundary with 2.56° misorientation where slip bands pass through and form small steps. Subfigure (d) shows microbands in 6Cr steel. All TEM micrographs have a 500 nm scale bar. The figure confirms that inconsistent deformation between austenite and ferrite leads to dislocation entanglement at phase boundaries, and slip bands can penetrate low-angle grain boundaries at high strain, supporting analysis of Cr addition effects on steel deformation mechanisms.
